context:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Syllabus:
11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning

Course Meeting Times:
- Seminar: 2 sessions/week; 1.5 hours/session

Course Description:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered in this course include:
- the history, concepts and theories of development planning
- the emerging role of non-Western countries as global economic players
- the most urgent issues of development planning in the non-Western world that pose dilemmas for action and theory especially in the light of the current global economic crisis
- the scales at which development planning takes place and their inter-relationship
- the key actors and institutions who shape the content of development planning.

The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Several themes run through the course. One such theme is the intimate link between planning, power and legitimacy, chiefly embodied in the state. Planning involves many actors, but always some governing authority with legitimacy. The approach to planning in this course involves the state as a governing authority, with the legitimate power to make changes that affect many other actors, in its unique role as the 'rule-maker' that determines the institutional framework for the operation of all other actors, while recognizing the growth of other governing actors at the local and global levels. Power also determines whether countries are able to plan, and if so, the kind of planning that they are able to do in a contested global arena. The language of legitimacy shapes how and by whom and in what form power can be exercised.

Another major theme of the course is that planning in developing countries is much more impacted by the ideas and actions of external actors, compared to planning in rich industrialized countries. This in turn makes the process of planning – the actors and institutional processes as well political sustainability – much more complex and unpredictable. The course also focuses on the history of planning and development in non-Western countries in order to better appreciate the achievements and limitations of planning efforts in the past. The impact of colonialism and imperialism on the political culture, institutional legacies, and economic prospects of developing countries needs to be understood. In focusing on this issue, the course would like to call attention to the importance of historical analysis in planning in developing countries.

Prerequisites:
There are no prerequisites for this course.

Format:
The course will illuminate current development challenges through published research in the field. The literature is rich, and across many disciplines in the social sciences. Case studies and real world examples, through interaction with planning practitioners, are drawn from around the world. The readings marked 'required' must be read carefully prior to class. Students are encouraged to prepare short outlines based on reading notes before coming to class. The readings marked 'recommended' are suggested to supplement, deepen and expand the issues raised by the required readings. While students don't have to read them all before class, they are strongly encouraged to read what they can in order to fully realize the potential of this course.

Requirements:

| Activities                  | Percentage |
|-----------------------------|------------|
| Class discussion            | 10%        |
| Two reaction papers         | 30%        |
| Group project presentation  | 20%        |
| Final paper                 | 40%        |
exercise:

Exercise:
TITLE: Actors: Civil Society: NGOs and Social Movements

11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning
Massachusetts Institute of Technology

Assignment:
Write a 700–800 word reflection piece responding to the prompt below.

Prompt:
There is no doubt about the mission of NGOs for promoting development. How good are NGOs as development agents? In what circumstances are they more effective as agents of development? What are their limitations? What is the role of other civil society actors in development such as social movements? What is the boundary of civil society as an actor in particular development contexts?

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: hi, I’m stuck on this reflection abt NGOs and social movements in development 😅 idk how to answer the “how good are NGOs as development agents” part without sounding too simplistic.
exchanges.tutor: Good question to start with. Before deciding whether NGOs are 'good' development agents, what do you think makes any actor a good development agent? What criteria would you use to judge that?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is starting the assignment and has a reasonable concern about depth. I should acknowledge their worry briefly, then use a Socratic question to get them thinking critically rather than giving them a framework. I want to prompt them to think about what 'good' even means as a development agent, which will naturally lead them toward a more nuanced answer.

exchanges.student: maybe stuff like whether they actually improve people’s lives, whether change lasts, and whether local ppl have a real say? idk if accountability should be a main criterion too.
exchanges.tutor: Those are solid criteria. Now apply them — how do NGOs actually perform against those standards? Think about what you've read. Do they tend to score well or poorly on, say, accountability or sustainability?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has come up with solid criteria on their own: impact, sustainability, participation, and accountability. I should validate these briefly and then push them to apply these criteria to NGOs specifically, rather than just listing abstract criteria. This moves the discussion toward the actual prompt.

exchanges.student: tbh I think NGOs score kinda mixed: maybe good at reaching ppl fast and trying local projects, but weaker on accountability since they answer to donors a lot. sustainability also seems shaky if the project depends on outside funding.
exchanges.tutor: Good, that donor-accountability tension is a key insight. So given that tension, under what circumstances do you think NGOs would actually be *more* effective as development agents?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is making good progress with a nuanced view. They've identified a real tension around donor accountability vs. local accountability. I should push them to think deeper about the circumstances under which NGOs might be more or less effective, which directly addresses the next part of the prompt.

exchanges.student: maybe when the state is weak or absent, or when NGOs fill a really specific gap like health, education, or disaster relief? also probs when they work w/ local groups instead of just dropping in.
exchanges.tutor: That's a strong point about working *with* local groups. Now flip it — what happens when NGOs *don't* work that way? And how do you think social movements differ from NGOs in that regard?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is building a coherent argument. They've identified state weakness and complementarity with local groups as conditions for NGO effectiveness. This is good but I should push them to also think about the flip side - when these conditions are absent - and then move them toward the next part of the prompt about social movements, which they haven't touched yet.

exchanges.student: if they don’t, it starts feeling top-down and maybe disconnected from what ppl actually need. social movements seem diff bc they come more from below and can push for structural change, not just service delivery.
exchanges.tutor: That bottom-up vs. top-down distinction is important. Now, the prompt also asks about the *boundary* of civil society as an actor — what do you think limits what civil society (NGOs and social movements together) can actually do in a given development context?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has made a crucial distinction between NGOs (service delivery, potentially top-down) and social movements (bottom-up, structural change). This is excellent progress. I should affirm this and push them to think about the last part of the prompt - the boundary of civil society as an actor in particular development contexts - which they haven't addressed yet.

exchanges.student: I’m guessing the big limits are state power, funding, and political repression. like civil society can advocate or deliver services, but it can’t fully replace the state or control the wider policy environment.
exchanges.tutor: You've now touched on all the key parts of the prompt. Before writing, can you tell me in one or two sentences what the central argument of your reflection piece would be? What's the main claim you want a reader to walk away with?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has covered all the major components of the prompt now: NGO effectiveness, conditions for effectiveness, limitations, social movements, and civil society boundaries. This is a good moment to ask them to step back and think about how all these pieces connect into a coherent argument for their reflection piece, rather than continuing to discuss individual points.

exchanges.student: maybe my main claim is that NGOs are useful but uneven development agents: they work best in specific, locally grounded roles, but they’re limited by donor dependence and weak accountability. and social movements matter bc they can build pressure for deeper political change that NGOs alone usually can’t deliver.
exchanges.tutor: That's a coherent central argument with real analytical bite. You have your pieces — now it's time to write. Start a draft and come back if you get stuck on a specific part.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has a solid central argument that ties together the key themes they've developed. This is a good moment to push them to start writing rather than continuing to refine orally. I should affirm the argument briefly and nudge them toward drafting, reminding them of the word count and that all the pieces are there.

exchanges.student: ok yeah, I think I can start now — ty. if I get stuck on the intro wording, I’ll ask.
exchanges.tutor: Good luck! Just a heads-up though — if you come back, I can help you think through *ideas*, but I won't suggest or edit any wording. The writing has to be yours. Go for it!
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is about to start writing, which is great. However, they've hinted they might come back asking for help with wording, which would cross into writing/editing territory that I must refuse. I should proactively set that boundary clearly but encouragingly before they go.

exchanges.student: got it, that’s fair. I’ll try drafting it myself first.
exchanges.tutor: Perfect. Come back once you have a draft or if you hit a conceptual wall. Good luck!
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is ready to write and has acknowledged the boundary. A brief, encouraging closing is appropriate here. No need to add more content.

exchanges.tutor: Anytime! See you when you have a draft. 👍
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: This is just a social closing message. A brief, friendly acknowledgment is appropriate.
